title: Data Pipeline (POC)
body:
We need to complete a POC of the data pipeline to prove feasibility.

All scripts for the POC pipeline can be dummy/simple notebooks which just output data/models to intermediate directory which we will improve on later.

The cloud infa/deployment will come later.

### High-Level System Architecture: "The Feedback Loop"

The system is designed as a closed-loop architecture. It doesn't just assign tickets; it learns from the outcome of those assignments to improve future accuracy.
1. The Multi-Modal Retrieval Engine

When a ticket arrives, the Matching Service performs two parallel lookups in PostgreSQL (pgvector):

    Semantic Path (Vector): Uses the Sentence Transformer to find engineers whose "profile centroid" is conceptually close to the ticket's problem domain (e.g., "Networking," "Security").

    Lexical Path (Keyword): Uses Full-Text Search to find exact matches for tools and error codes (e.g., "Terraform," "404," "EKS").

    Fusion: Both results are merged using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) to produce the final Top-5 list.

2. The Living Profile (The Learning Mechanism)

Engineer profiles are not static.

    Initialization: Profiles start from Resume Embeddings (Cold Start).

    Evolution: As tickets are closed, a Background Worker triggers a "Profile Update."

    Decay Function: Recent tickets have a higher weight than older ones, allowing the system to track an engineer's current focus rather than what they did three years ago.

### Data Contract
Postgres Tables:

#### User Table

| Column Name          | Type        | Description                                                          |
| -------------------- | ----------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| member_id            | UUID (PK)   | Primary key identifying the engineer.                                |
| full_name            | String      | Engineer’s full name (display purposes).                             |
| resume_base_vector   | vector(384) | Original embedding generated from the engineer’s resume.             |
| profile_vector       | vector(384) | Dynamic centroid embedding updated as tickets are completed.         |
| skill_keywords       | tsvector    | Weighted keywords (e.g., Kubernetes, AWS) for hybrid keyword search. |
| tickets_closed_count | Integer     | Number of tickets closed; used to weight profile confidence.         |



#### Ticket Table
| Column Name            | Type               | Description                                                        |
| ---------------------- | ------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| ticket_id              | UUID / String (PK) | Original ticket ID from GitHub.                            |
| title                  | Text               | Ticket title for keyword search.                                   |
| description            | Text               | Full ticket description for keyword search.                        |
| ticket_vector          | vector(384)        | Semantic embedding of the ticket content.                          |
| labels                 | JSONB              | Array of tags (e.g., `bug`, `enhancement`, `priority:high`).       |
| status                 | Enum               | Ticket state: `open`, `in-progress`, `closed`.                     |
| resolution_time_actual | Interval           | Ground truth resolution time used to train the regressor (TF-204). |

#### Assignments Table
| Column Name   | Type      | Description                                    |
| ------------- | --------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| assignment_id | UUID (PK) | Primary key for the assignment record.         |
| ticket_id     | FK        | Foreign key referencing `tickets.ticket_id`.   |
| engineer_id   | FK        | Foreign key referencing `engineers.member_id`. |
| assigned_at   | Timestamp | Time the ticket was assigned to the engineer.  |


Work with each other to determine data contract during the ETL steps.

### System Layout
```
[ EXTERNAL SYSTEMS ]           [ TICKETFORGE CORE ]          [ STORAGE ]
      
      +----------------+             +-------------------+       +----------------+
      |  GitHub/Jira   | --Ticket--> | Hybrid Matcher    | <---> |  PostgreSQL    |
      |  (New Issues)  |             | (Vector + BM25)   |       |   + pgvector   |
      +-------^--------+             +---------+---------+       +-------+--------+
              |                                |                         |
              |                         [ Suggested ]                    |
              |                         [ Assignees ]                    |
              |                                |                         |
      +-------+--------+             +---------v---------+               |
      |  Engineering   |             |  Human-in-Loop    |               |
      |     Team       | <---------- |  (Triage/Accept)  |               |
      +-------+--------+             +---------+---------+               |
              |                                |                         |
              |                        [ Work Starts ]                   |
              |                                |                         |
      +-------v--------+             +---------v---------+               |
      |  Jira Status:  |             |  Profile Updater  |               |
      |    "CLOSED"    | ----------> | (Moving Average)  | --------------+
      +----------------+             +-------------------+       (Updates Vector
                                                                  & Keywords)
```

### Definition of Done (DoD)

  Code Quality: All Python code passes ruff linter, has at least one test for each feature, and passes `ty` or `pyright` static analysis (TBD). All functions should be typed, and have some form of documentation.

  Model Performance:

      #8 : Embeddings are generated in <100ms.

      #9 : Hybrid search returns the "Actual Assignee" in the Top 5 for at least 70% of historical test data.

      #10  : Resolution time MAE is <8 hours.

  Integration: The Profile Updater successfully recalculates the engineer's vector and increments keywords in Postgres after a simulated "Close" event (i.e. replaying).

  Documentation: The README.md is updated with instructions on how to run the ETL pipeline as well as any other scripts (i.e. linting, testing, data scraping, etc.)

  Persistence: Profiles and ticket embeddings are successfully stored in pgvector and can survive a container restart.

title: Transform: Ticket - Data Normalization + Feature Engineering
body:
This step will load the compressed json from the previous step as input.

Ultimately, we want to be able to query a vector representation of the content of each of these tickets. To do this, we have to employ text cleaning/normalization and vectorization. First, we will employ text normalization techniques such as removing markdown syntax, image links, and truncating large code snippets (i.e. take first 2 lines and last 2 lines of code block since this may help embedding). 

We also need to do feature engineering to extract more value from the input fields. For instance, we can enrich our data with the completion time of a ticket, but doing this requires computing this new field from the createdAt and the closedAt  fields and then doing logic to account for business days (so as to not penalize weekends). Similarly, we can map engineer seniority to an enumeration, compute historical average completion speed of tickets (perhaps weighted average for most recent entries), or even the story point load the engineer already has.

finally embed using embedder (#8) and extract keywords (#11). Make sure to save this information into intermediate file.

For this part, we need to load that extracted text, perform normalization (stemming optional for some embeddings, but can investigate removal of things like addresses, phone numbers, dates, GPA, etc.).

Finally, embed using embedder (#8) and extract keywords (#11). this step should output an intermediate file.

title: CoreML: Continous Engineer Profile Learning
body:
This ticket implements the "Profile Update" functionality that triggers upon a ticket being marked as 'Closed'.

--- PART 1 (embedding)
Use the resume and ticket embeddings to create the engineer embedding. This engineer embedding is used to match an engineer to a ticket using cosine similarity.

NOTE: the resume and ticket embeddings have to be created using the same embedding model (must be embeded into the same embedding space for this to work!)

We use this resume embedding as a “Base” and then as an engineer completes tickets we update the engineer embedding using a weighted average. The exact equation is up to you, but I suggest something along the lines of

$\alpha*\text{old profile embedding}+(1-\alpha)*\text{just completed ticket embedding}$
where $\alpha=.9)

--- PART 2 (keyword search)
Initialize base keywords for each engineer from resume, or empty set if none. Then, as tickets are closed, take the keywords from the ticket, and add to the engineer keywords with a count/weight

Scenario 1: live updates work
Given: A completed ticket with a specific embedding and set of extracted keywords.
When: The ticket status changes to Done or Closed.
Then:
    Update Vector: Re-calculate the engineer's centroid vector using a decay-weighted average (as shown above in part 1).
    Update Keywords: Increment the frequency count of the ticket's keywords in the engineer's profile (as shown in part 2).
    Sync DB: Save the updated tsvector and embedding back to the engineers table.

title: CoreML: Hybrid Recommendation Engine
body:
Implement a hybrid retrieval system using pgvector (#7.)for semantic similarity and PostgreSQL Full-Text Search (TSVECTOR/BM25) for keyword matching. To do this, you will need to using something like Reciprocal Rank Fusion,.


Scenario 1: general case
**Given**: A new ticket and the existing database of engineer profiles/historical tickets.
**When**: The system executes a search using both a vector embedding (Cosine Similarity) and a keyword query (BM25/Lexical).
**Then**: The system uses Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) to merge the two ranked lists into a single "Top 5" list that balances conceptual expertise with exact keyword matches.

Scenario 2: more specific
**Given**: A new ticket (e.g., "Fix EKS Ingress controller 404 errors") and a list of engineer profiles.
**When**: 
  1.  Vector Search: Finds engineers with general "Kubernetes networking" experience.
  2.  Keyword Search: Specifically looks for engineers whose resumes/history contain "EKS" and "Ingress".
  3.  Merge: The system applies Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) to score the intersection.
**Then**: An engineer who has both the conceptual knowledge (vector) and the specific tool experience (keyword) is ranked first.

title: Transform: Keyword Extractor
body:
To optimize our matching, we need to supplement vector match with keyword search.

Why?
- For Resumes: You run the extractor once during onboarding (or when the resume is updated). These keywords become the engineer's Permanent Skill Tags.
- For Tickets: You run the extractor on every incoming ticket (title + body). These become the Required Skill Tags for that specific task. For completed tickets that an engineer does, we also re-rerank their skills using this.

What does it look like?
"Fix Kubernetes ingress timeout on AWS." → [Kubernetes, AWS]

Implementation: Can vary based on what you think is appropriate.
I think an easy approach is to generate a fixed list of known skill terms (i.e. http, api, spring, golang, etc.).
If you do research and find a better approach, please make discussion in this comment, the team, or me.

Acceptance criteria:
 Given: text blob
 When: run through this script
 Then: outputs a list of the keywords we detected

title: CoreML: Bootstrapping
body:
Data ingestion needs to simulate "learning"/upskilling of engineers (i.e. the continous profile updates #6 , #12 ) for each engineer.

As such, we cannot just ingest the history as a flat file. We must "replay" the history of tickets by looking at their closing date and so on to build the correct state and experience due to the way we compute a weighted average.

The ETL process adds all the engineers, tickets, and assignments as tables. Thus, we can bootstrap by looking at tickets we imported into our DB for keyword/vectors, and then applying the function implemented in #6 to update the vectors and keywords of the engineer. We apply the updates chronologically for each engineer. In our actual system, we would use an update queue. 



    Given: a list of tickets which have closed
    When: Running the initial ETL.
    Then: The pipeline should process the tickets in the order they are recieved, updating the team member profiles after each ticket to simulate how the model would have learned over the past year.

title: Platform: [py] setup linting and type checking
body:
Like the title says, setup linting (using `ruff`), type checking (using `pyright` or `ty`), and test running with pytest with documentation and scripts to run them.

Make sure to add all of these tools as `dev` dependencies.

I highly recommend the following setup in the repo for `ruff` linting:

```
[tool.ruff]
line-length = 88
indent-width = 2
target-version = "py313"
[tool.ruff.lint.pydocstyle]
convention = "google" 

[tool.ruff.lint.flake8-tidy-imports]
ban-relative-imports = "all"

[tool.ruff.lint]
select = [
    "D", 
    "E", 
    "F", 
    "ANN", 
    "B", 
    "A", 
    "LOG", 
    "R", 
    "EM",
    "R",
    "Q0",
    "I",
    "N",
    "TRY",
    "TID",
    "PLR"
]
ignore = ["D104", "D100", "D205", "TRY002"]

fixable = ["ALL"]
unfixable = []

dummy-variable-rgx = "^(_+|(_+[a-zA-Z0-9_]*[a-zA-Z0-9]+?))$"

[tool.pytest.ini_options]
pythonpath = ["."]
timeout = 10
```

For static checking, here is a config I use with mypy, but I recommend other static analsysi tool:

```
[tool.mypy]
strict = true
disallow_untyped_defs = true
namespace_packages = true
explicit_package_bases = true
mypy_path = "stubs"
follow_untyped_imports = true
disallow_untyped_calls = false
disable_error_code = ["unused-ignore"]
```

title: Platform: Monorepo CI/CD
body:
Implement a GitHub Actions workflow that performs linting, type-checking, and testing, triggered only for the specific sub-directories modified in a Pull Request. Why - because otherwise the CI would run forever and eat up all our credits.

This can be done using tools like turborepo, or with crude path logic in the github action file:

Path Logic for python stuff: * 
    If libs/* changes → Test apps/training and apps/web-backend.
    If libs/shared changes → Test all Python apps.
    If apps/web-backend changes → Test only web-backend.

Path Logic for node stuff: Only triggers if files in apps/web-frontend or root package.json change.

Acceptance Criteria

    Given: A Python file in libs/ is modified.
    When: The CI runs.
    Then: ruff checks the whole workspace, but pytest runs specifically for the python apps

    Given: A syntax error or type mismatch is introduced.
    When: ruff or mypy executes.
    Then: The pipeline fails immediately before running expensive test suite

    Given: Changes to shared dependencies or root-level configuration files.
    When: The CI pipeline is triggered.
    Then: Both the Backend and Frontend suites run to ensure no breaking cross-package changes.

    Given: modification to frontend code
    When: The CI runs.
    Then: npm run lint checks style, npm run type-check (tsc) ensures no TS errors, and npm test runs the unit tests.
    
    Given: A change is made to apps/training (Python only).
    When: The CI runs.
    Then: The Frontend CI pipeline is skipped entirely to save credits/time.

body: Please add all the logic for bias detection as detailed in the requirements doc: 1. Detecting Bias in Your Data: To ensure that your data is not biased, you need to perform data slicing and analyze performance across different subgroups. Identify demographic or categorical features in your data (such as age, gender, location, etc.) and evaluate how your model behaves for each of these subgroups. 2. Data Slicing for Bias Analysis: Use tools such as SliceFinder, TensorFlow Model Analysis (TFMA), or Fairlearn to implement data slicing tech- niques. These tools can help you split your data into meaningful slices and track model performance on each slice. This helps you determine whether the model is biased toward or against certain groups. 3. Mitigation of Bias: If you detect any significant performance differ- ences across slices, you must take steps to mitigate bias. Techniques such as re-sampling underrepresented groups, applying fairness constraints, or adjusting decision thresholds can be used to address this. 4. Document Bias Mitigation Process: Clearly document the steps you took to detect and mitigate bias. Include explanations of the types of bias found, how you addressed them, and whether any trade-offs were made in terms of overall performance.

title: Model Development: Code for Bias Detection
body:
Document Requirements 2.4 and 2.5

Evaluate the model on different slices of the data to detect bias. Use Fairfearn or Tensorflow Model Analysis (TFMA)

https://github.com/ALearningCurve/ticket-forge/blob/main/apps/training/training/trainers/utils/harness.py#L148

title: Model Development: Hyper param search
body:
Requirement 3.1 of the source document

Do hyper param search with cross validation, make sure the best model is saved with the actual best hyperparam results!

title: Experiment Tracking: tools and results
body: From section 4 of the requirements document - **tracking tools**: use MLFlow (preferred) or Weights and Biases to track your experiments and results. Runs should log hyperparams and model performance/versions - **results**: provide visualizations or images of breakdown of results. these should highlight comparison of model. We currently already have a plot being made (https://github.com/ALearningCurve/ticket-forge/blob/main/apps/training/training/cmd/train.py#L150) but this can be expanded (sameer may have done visualizations - check with him to see if there are any gaps) This mlflow implementation has to track - Performance of each model type (random forest, xgboost, etc.) and performance of each hyperparam trial (see below screenshot) - Have ability to automatically "promote" the best model I recently implemented something like this for a lab in this class: https://github.com/ALearningCurve/ie7343-mlops-lab4

title: Model Sensititivity Aanlysis
body: From section 5 of the requirements document. Perform sensitivity analysis to determine how the model's performance changes with respect to different - **feature importance** for the selected model (SHAP or LIME) to understand how features impact model predictions - **Hyperparam sensitivity** analyze how changes in hyperparameters affect model performance. Particularly intested in which hyperparameters have the biggest affect on outcomes For the hyperparam, you can simply load the Sklearn gridsearch pickle or the results json to create a graphic after the training script runs. In the past I have done something similar for hyperparam sensitivity by plotting performance on y axis and the hyperparam value on the x axis for each training run:

title: Model Bias Deteciton
body:
Important to assert that the model is not biased against any subgroup

See section 6 of the requirements document as well as sections 2.4 and 2.5.

Evaluate the model on different slices of the data to detect bias. Use Fairfearn or Tensorflow Model Analysis (TFMA). We want to detect, apply automatic remediations, and document bias remediation.

Note that this has already been partially implemented: https://github.com/ALearningCurve/ticket-forge/blob/main/apps/training/training/trainers/utils/harness.py#L211

title: CI/CD pipeline and evaluation
body:
Implement section 7 of the requirements document.

We will use github actions for the model ci/cd. In particular, you can run a action which does the training, grabs the latest data from DVC, and then promotes the 'best model' (set the model to production in mlflow). 

This workflow should trigger on merge to main & on a weekly cron.

Please reuse existing functionality for things like email alerts (ask sameer) and creating model artifact (#76).

title: not to be merged with main - Model Evaluation and Inference Analysis
body:
Description:
Now that the best model is trained, validated, and pushed to GCP, we want to understand how it behaves in practice. **This ticket is purely exploratory and the output will not be merged to main.**
Goals:

- Load the best model from the local run directory and run inference on sample tickets from the test set
- Inspect what a raw ticket looks like at each stage of the pipeline — raw text, normalized text, extracted keywords, and the final 384-dim embedding
- Evaluate predictions on the held-out test set — plot actual vs predicted completion times and residuals to understand where the model performs well and where it struggles
- Understand the prediction distribution — are predictions clustered around a mean, are there outliers, how does the model handle very short vs very long tickets
- Run end-to-end inference on a brand new raw ticket — take raw text, normalize it, embed it, and output a predicted completion time in business hours

Implementation:
A standalone analysis script or Jupyter notebook under apps/training/training/analysis/ or notebooks/. **This will not be merged to main — a draft PR will be opened for team visibility.**

Definition of Done:

- End-to-end inference works on a new raw ticket
- Actual vs predicted plot generated on the test set
- Residual distribution plot generated
- Findings documented in the PR description

title: feat: Improve Model Performance on Real Data
body:
Description:
After evaluating the model on real data(#88), the current models perform poorly:
* R2: -0.16 (worse than predicting the mean)
* MAE: 234 hours
* Predictions compressed into 187-616 hour range regardless of actual ticket complexity
Root Causes Identified:
* Target variable completion_hours_business has extreme variance and a heavy long tail (0 to 2960 hours)
* All seniority labels are mid due to heuristic limitation — no variation for the model to learn from
* Outlier tickets (1000+ hours) dominate the loss and confuse training


Proposed Improvements:
1. Log-transform the target variable to reduce the impact of extreme outliers
2. Filter or cap outlier completion times before training
3. Fix seniority labels to reflect actual engineer experience
4. Add more engineered features beyond raw embeddings (e.g. label encoding, repo encoding, ticket length)
5. Experiment with larger n_iter in RandomizedSearchCV for better hyperparameter coverage

title: feat: Improve Model Generalization and Reframe Prediction to Time Buckets
body:
## Description

Following PR #88, models fail to generalize to unseen data despite strong debug performance. The root cause is poor target formulation and data quality issues. This ticket reframes the problem as classification instead of regression.

---

## Scope of Work

1. Re-run Airflow pipeline with fresh dataset, resampling, and sample weights
2. Drop records with negative `completion_hours_business` — no imputation
3. Replace regression target with 4 time buckets (S/M/L/XL)
4. Switch evaluation metrics to Accuracy and Macro F1
5. Remove TF-IDF, BoW, and seniority_enum from features
6. Add time-to-assignment as a priority signal
7. Train and compare RF, XGBoost, and LightGBM
8. Update CI/CD gates for classification metrics
9. Report per-repo bias analysis

---

## Acceptance Criteria

- No mean imputation in training pipeline
- Target converted to time buckets
- R² and MAE removed
- TF-IDF and seniority_enum removed
- Model retrained and logged in MLflow
- CI/CD gates updated for classification
- Bias analysis reported per repo

---

title: fix: correct XGBoost num_class and add prediction confidence scores
body:
# fix: correct XGBoost num_class and add prediction confidence scores

## Problem

Two issues found during local testing and code review of the classification pipeline:

1. `XGBClassifier` was configured with `num_class=6` but the model only has 4 classes (S/M/L/XL buckets: 0–10, 10–50, 50–100, 100–480 business hours). This was introduced when switching from regression to classification in #111.

2. No interpretability metrics were being tracked — there was no way to know how confident the model was in its predictions.

## Changes

- Fixed `num_class=6` → `num_class=4` in `train_xgboost.py`
- Added `mean_confidence` and `low_confidence_rate` to eval metrics in `harness.py` using `predict_proba`. `low_confidence_rate` tracks predictions below 50% confidence — these can be flagged for human review in future work.
- Added 3 unit tests covering confidence score computation, low confidence detection, and fallback when `predict_proba` is unavailable

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `num_class=4` in XGBoost config
- [ ] `mean_confidence` and `low_confidence_rate` present in `eval_{model}.json`
- [ ] 3 new tests passing
- [ ] `just check` passes

title: demo: semantic search
body:
A key feature of our product is the [Engineer <-> Ticket] matching system

There are a couple key tenants:
1. when clicking an engineer profile, it should recommend tickets well suited for that engineer as well as tickets which the engineer has capacity for
2. when clicking a ticket, it should recommend engineers well suited for that task
3. when closing a ticket (i.e. moving to complete), update the engineer(s) profile who was assigned that ticket with the ticket information


The logic for semantic search and the profile updating already exists in the app, there just needs to be API routes for this + UI integration

title: demo: resume upload working
body:
the resume upload page of the web app should actually do a profile update (page exists but needs to be hooked up to do something). This logic already exists in the app as part of airflow DAG, but feel free to reimplement the embedding/label logic in the web backend for simplicity (i.e. one less service).

Given GCP AZ issues with compute engine, it might make sense to just re-implement this logic in the web backend or put the profile update logic in the ml-core lib which is shared between dags and backend

title: feat: rebuild dataset with clean, expanded ticket corpus
body:
## Motivation
The current dataset has two major issues:

- Data leakage — upsampling happens before the train/test split, causing 54.68% of test keys to appear in training. This inflates our metrics significantly.
- Low quality tickets — the corpus includes tickets without assignees, open tickets, and tickets from before 2020 that may not reflect modern DevOps practices.

## Proposed Changes

- Remove upsampling entirely — use sample weights during training instead to handle class imbalance
- Add more open source repos — expand beyond Terraform, Ansible, Prometheus to increase dataset diversity and reduce overfitting
- Filter tickets to only include:

-- Closed tickets with an assignee
-- Created after January 1, 2020

-Split before any weighting — ensure train/validation/test split happens on raw data with no leakage

## Expected Outcome

- Cleaner train/test split with near-zero leakage (target < 1%)
- More diverse ticket corpus reducing repo-specific bias
- Honest model metrics that reflect real-world performance
- Better generalization across ticket types
